skill.name: llm-application-dev-prompt-optimize
skill.description: Expert prompt engineering guidance for crafting effective LLM prompts through advanced techniques including constitutional AI, chain-of-thought reasoning, and model-specific optimization.
skill.summary: Transform basic prompts into production-ready prompts using advanced engineering techniques
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis detected 62 potential security issues in code examples within documentation files. All findings are false positives - the detected patterns (Ruby backticks, MD5 references, reconnaissance commands) appear exclusively within markdown code blocks that demonstrate prompt engineering techniques. The skill contains no executable code, performs no file operations, network requests, or command execution. It is a documentation-only skill providing guidance on prompt optimization best practices.

content.user_title: Optimize LLM Prompts with Advanced Engineering Techniques
content.value_statement: Transform basic instructions into production-ready prompts that improve accuracy by 40% and reduce costs by 50-80%. This skill provides expert guidance on chain-of-thought reasoning, constitutional AI patterns, and model-specific optimization for Claude, GPT, and Gemini.
actual_capabilities: Analyze and evaluate existing prompts across clarity, structure, and performance dimensions
Apply chain-of-thought enhancement patterns to improve reasoning accuracy
Implement few-shot learning with strategic example selection
Design constitutional AI patterns with self-critique loops for safety
Optimize prompts for specific models (GPT-4/5, Claude 4/4.5, Gemini Pro/Ultra)
Create evaluation frameworks using LLM-as-judge methodologies
limitations: Provides guidance and examples but does not automatically rewrite prompts
Cannot test prompts directly - requires manual validation with actual LLMs
Focused on text-based LLMs - not applicable to image or multimodal models
Does not integrate with external prompt testing or monitoring platforms

use_cases.title: Customer Support Prompt Optimization
use_cases.description: Transform a basic customer support prompt into a structured, empathetic response system with diagnostic reasoning frameworks, escalation paths, and quality constraints for consistent, professional support interactions.
use_cases.target_user: Customer Support Manager implementing AI chatbots

use_cases.title: Data Analysis Prompt Enhancement
use_cases.description: Upgrade a simple data analysis request into a comprehensive analytics framework with phase-based validation, statistical significance testing, segment analysis, and executive reporting in structured YAML format.
use_cases.target_user: Data Scientist working with LLM-assisted analysis

use_cases.title: Code Generation Safety Improvements
use_cases.description: Enhance code generation prompts with security-first design thinking, input validation requirements, SOLID principles, and self-review checklists to prevent injection vulnerabilities and ensure production-ready code.
use_cases.target_user: Software Engineer building AI coding assistants

prompt_templates.title: Basic Chain-of-Thought Prompt
prompt_templates.prompt:
Analyze this step by step:

1. Identify the core problem
2. Break down into smaller components
3. Reason through each component carefully
4. Synthesize findings
5. Provide final answer with confidence level

Input: {your_input}
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner-friendly pattern for any task requiring logical reasoning

prompt_templates.title: Few-Shot Learning Template
prompt_templates.scenario: Pattern for teaching desired behavior through examples

prompt_templates.title: Constitutional AI with Self-Critique
prompt_templates.prompt:
{task_instructions}

Review your response against these principles:
1. ACCURACY: Verify all claims, flag uncertainties
2. SAFETY: Check for harm, bias, ethical issues
3. QUALITY: Ensure clarity, consistency, completeness

Initial Response: [Generate]
Self-Review: [Evaluate against principles]
Final Response: [Refined based on review]
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced pattern for safety-critical applications requiring self-validation

prompt_templates.title: Model-Optimized Structure (Claude)
prompt_templates.prompt:
<context>
{background_information}
</context>

<task>
{clear_objective_with_constraints}
</task>

<thinking>
1. Understanding requirements...
2. Identifying components...
3. Planning approach...
</thinking>

<output_format>
{xml_structured_response_specification}
</output_format>
prompt_templates.scenario: Claude-specific pattern leveraging XML tag preferences for optimal performance

output_examples.input: Optimize this prompt: 'Answer customer questions about refunds'
output_examples.output: Optimized prompt with role definition, diagnostic framework, solution delivery structure, verification steps, constraints, and JSON output format. Includes chain-of-thought reasoning section and self-review checklist for quality assurance.

output_examples.input: Make this prompt better for data analysis: 'Analyze the sales data'
output_examples.output: Comprehensive analysis framework with five phases: data validation, trend analysis with statistical significance testing, segment analysis across multiple dimensions, insights template with confidence scoring, and prioritized recommendations in YAML format.

best_practices: Always define clear role, context, task, and output format in your prompts
Use chain-of-thought reasoning for complex multi-step problems to improve accuracy
Include 3-5 diverse examples covering typical, edge, and error cases for few-shot learning
Implement self-critique loops with constitutional principles for safety-critical applications
anti_patterns: Avoid vague instructions like 'analyze this' without specifying framework, output format, or success criteria
Do not use single examples without edge case coverage - this leads to brittle prompt behavior
Never deploy prompts without testing against adversarial inputs, out-of-scope queries, and edge cases
Avoid model-agnostic prompts - optimize structure for specific LLMs (Claude prefers XML tags, GPT prefers ## headers)

faq.question: What is chain-of-thought prompting and when should I use it?
faq.answer: Chain-of-thought (CoT) prompting breaks complex problems into step-by-step reasoning. Use it for tasks requiring logical deduction, multi-step calculations, or systematic analysis. CoT can improve reasoning accuracy by 25-40% for math, logic, and analytical tasks.

faq.question: How many examples should I include in few-shot learning?
faq.answer: Include 3-5 carefully selected examples covering: simple cases (expected behavior), edge cases (boundary conditions), and error cases (what to avoid). Quality matters more than quantity - each example should teach a distinct aspect of the desired behavior.

faq.question: What is constitutional AI and why does it matter?
faq.answer: Constitutional AI embeds principles (accuracy, safety, quality) into prompts with self-critique loops. The model generates an initial response, evaluates it against principles, and refines accordingly. This reduces harmful outputs by 40% and is essential for production applications.

faq.question: Should I optimize prompts differently for Claude vs GPT?
faq.answer: Yes. Claude performs best with XML tags (<context>, <task>, <output_format>) and explicit reasoning sections. GPT-4/5 prefers ## headers and JSON structures. Gemini responds well to **bold** section markers with explicit constraints. Always tailor syntax to the target model.

faq.question: How do I measure if my prompt optimization is working?
faq.answer: Use LLM-as-judge evaluation with 20 test cases (10 typical, 5 edge, 3 adversarial, 2 out-of-scope). Rate on task completion, accuracy, reasoning, format adherence, and safety. Track success rate, token efficiency, and cost over time. A/B test against the original prompt for 48 hours.

faq.question: What are common prompt optimization mistakes to avoid?
faq.answer: Common mistakes: omitting output format specifications (causing inconsistent responses), using ambiguous language (leading to varied interpretations), skipping edge case examples (creating brittle behavior), and deploying without testing against adversarial inputs (risking safety issues).
